text.fullText: UNITED STATES DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION FEDERAL MOTOR CARRIER SAFETY ADMINISTRATION  Transparency in Property Broker Transactions    Notice; Request for Public Comment Docket Number: FMCSA-2023-0257   COMMENTS SUBMITTED BY: CIRCLE LOGISTICS INC.      1950 W Cook Rd, Suite 102 Fort Wayne, IN 46818   Robert Fortmeyer   JANUARY 9, 2025     This comment is made on behalf of Circle Logistics Inc., a licensed interstate property broker under MC number 761291. Circle Logistics publishes this comment in opposition to the proposed rule because the rule is unnecessary, ineffective, and inconsistent with Congressional intent to deregulate transportation.  1. Lack of Economic Purpose  The FMCSA’s proposed rule is problematic on several levels, but first and foremost, it serves no economic purpose.  The FMCSA recognizes that the proposed rule is unlikely to have any impact on freight rates since the information that must be provided by the broker is only required to be provided after a transaction (within 48 hours of a request by a party to the shipment in question, after completion of that shipment).  Circle Logistics agrees with the FMCSA’s assessment that the proposed rule is unlikely to impact rates, and that market forces are much more likely to impact transportation rates.  The OOIDA and SBTC petitions for rulemaking, together with the comments from their members already filed with regard to FMCSA-2023-0257, focus on hypothetical broker margins and allegedly unfair transportation rates. The fact that the FMCSA recognizes that the proposed rule is unlikely to impact rates underscores just how unnecessary it is to amend the regulation.  The NPRM states, “FMCSA understands that broker records would be of limited utility in negotiating contracts due to the effect of the pricing factors listed above, as such records are provided only upon request and after the completion of the contractual obligations.”  Rulemaking, at 91665. The Agency further explains, “FMCSA believes the content of records under §371.3 would be of limited utility in negotiating rates.”  As such, transparency for the purpose of negotiations is not a sufficient justification for the proposed rule.  The NPRM also indicates transaction records may be helpful to remedy potential chargeback abuse or other erroneous charges on completed loads.  Rulemaking at 91663.  However, margin information is not necessary to substantiate a chargeback, and as drafted, the proposed rule would allow margin information on any completed shipment, even a shipment where no chargeback or rate dispute is present.  Further, the Agency seems to misunderstand the financial risk associated with brokerage.  The FMCSA continues to follow OOIDA and SBTC’s shipment-by-shipment assessment, ignoring the fact that brokers attempt to secure a significant portion of the shipper’s freight spend, not individual shipper shipments.  This can be done by breaking even (or even losing money) on one lane, while making a higher-than-normal margin on another lane.  Brokers often have contracted rates in place with shippers that do not change as frequently as the rates in the carrier spot market.  Additionally, shipper contracts are increasingly requiring brokers to take on cargo claim liability and to indemnify the shipper for anything that goes wrong with a shipment.  Thus, to secure the business of a specific shipper, a broker may be counting on a significant number of shipments, and it may be taking on significant exposure for cargo claims and indemnification of the shipper – furthermore, there is always a risk that the broker will pay the motor carrier but will not receive payment from the shipper.  This last scenario is worth further explanation.  In recent years, several major shippers filed for bankruptcy protection, leaving brokers who already paid their underlying motor carriers completely exposed to the non-payment. The Agency’s shipment-by-shipment analysis of profitability ignores the fact that the broker alone bears the risk of non-payment when it pays the motor carrier before being paid by the shipper as the brokers who lost millions in the above shipper bankruptcies can attest.  The shipment-by-shipment approach not only fails to adequately represent a broker’s true profit and expense on a business segment, but it also fails to provide a carrier with enough data to enable that carrier to effectively change its freight charges or develop a better understanding of market rates. The number of shipments that occur on any given day, in any given market, with varying commodities and shipper or receiver needs create a vast market with numerous variables that make developing an understanding of “market rates” nearly impossible. There are multiple firms in the transportation industry that buy freight charge data, aggregate that data, and sell it to brokers, carriers, and shippers to enable each transportation stakeholder to understand what the “market rate” for a given shipment should be. Despite the efforts of these widely used firms, there are near constant disputes from various stakeholders as to the accuracy of the data the firms sell. If the firms who have a primary business of selling aggregated freight charge data are not able to gather enough data to satisfy carriers with the knowledge of current market rates, it is likely impossible for a carrier to collect enough data to accurately calculate market rates.   Additionally, shippers often require brokers to provide non-brokerage services for their businesses.  For example, a shipper may require a broker to secure warehouse space and manage inventory as part of a fulfilment program, or  to provide a fleet of trailers (a trailer pool, as it is known in the industry), so the shipper can pre-load trailers for pickup by carriers.  The costs related to these non-brokerage services may be built into the brokerage rates.   Another unrepresented problem is that some shipments may include services from more than one carrier. For example, a shipment from Mexico to the United States may include a Mexican carrier that takes a shipment to the border, a transfer carrier that takes the shipment across the border, and a US carrier that takes the shipment to its final delivery. Similar situations occur when there are imports requiring drayage from a port to a warehouse and a different carrier to perform the over-the-road transportation, and other multi-modal shipments. As written, it appears that the Rule as amended would require disclosure of all charges incurred by a party to the shipment, including for transportation services not rendered by that carrier.  Brokers contractually take on shipper required risk, bear a disproportionate risk related to shipper non-payment (when compared to the carriers), make money on some shipper lanes and shipments while losing money on others, and often provide ancillary services, the cost of which may or may not be encapsulated in the shipment-by-shipment margin.    For these reasons, a shipment-by-shipment evaluation of broker margin does not accurately represent the broker’s risk, exposure, cost, or profit earned  in servicing its shipper-customer.  Since the FMCSA recognizes that this margin related information is unlikely to be helpful in the negotiation of carrier rates, there is no reason to require a broker to provide it, especially when the broker’s agreement with a motor carrier is for a fixed sum.  A motor carrier is offered a shipment at a specific price, and it accepts the proposed compensation and terms related thereto prior to transporting the shipment.  If the compensation offered is not appropriate, the motor carrier is not obligated to accept the shipment.  Given that the NPRM provides no compelling reason to require brokers to disclose rate information (other than the vague concept of transparency and fairness), and given that the Agency itself recognizes the lack of impact the proposed rule is anticipated to have on rates and negotiations going forward, the broker should not be required to disclose its  rate with its shipper-customer and its agreed payment to the motor carrier.  The information required to be disclosed does not take into account the broker’s overall profit with the shipper customer, other requirements the shipper may contractually impose on the broker (risk or required additional services), and there is no need to protect motor carriers from terms they accept prior to transporting shipments.    2. There is significant, underrepresented cost to brokers.  The NPRM also suggests that brokers can comply with the proposed rule with little expense.  This is simply not the case.  The NPRM indicates in several places that automatic disclosure is viewed as too burdensome on brokers, and we most certainly agree.  However, the NPRM does not recognize that disclosure upon request may be equally or more burdensome than automatic disclosure.  Since any party to the shipment can request the information under the proposed rule, the motor carrier and the shipper could request the information at different times.  Additionally, since the rule requires disclosure within 48 hours of a request, information requested immediately after delivery will most likely be incomplete.  Within 48 hours of delivery, the broker will not have been paid by its shipper, will not know if there are cargo claims or other offsets or penalties, and it probably will not have made payment to its motor carrier.  In fact, since most contracts allow the shipper nine (9) months to make a cargo claim, it is possible that the broker itself will not know of a cargo claim until nine (9) months after the date of delivery.  Additionally, there is no restriction on how the parties can request the information, so a broker will have to monitor mail, fax, text, WhatsApp, email, TMS messages, phone calls and voicemails, and all other means of communication for requests from shippers and carriers – and it will need to be staffed to respond to such requests within 48 hours.  With a minimum of two parties to every shipment, and the potential for multiple requests from each party on a single shipment (because information is not likely to be available immediately after delivery), the 48 hour response requirement and the suggestion that non-compliance could negatively impact the broker’s operating authority creates a very heavy, expensive, administrative burden.  All the required information is unlikely to be able to be gathered and provided within 48 hours after receiving a request, particularly if a request is submitted immediately after a shipment is delivered.  Further, the carrier argument that it received a clean bill of lading at destination and are therefore surprised by a cargo claim – and the fact that this argument is repeated in the NPRM, shows a fundamental misunderstanding of the Carmack Amendment, currently codified at 49 USC §14706.  For over 110 years a clean bill of lading has been prima facie evidence of delivery in good condition that can be disproven with any other evidence.  Brokers are often in the exact same position as carriers in this regard – i.e. the broker is often contractually liable to the shipper for cargo claims and surprised by a claim they were unaware of until filed by the shipper almost nine (9) months later.  This is more of a shipper/receiver issue than it is a broker/carrier issue, and the repeated reference to clean delivery receipts or bills of lading in the NPRM and the comments already filed is nothing more than a red herring.    In addition to the administrative burden and practicality of the availability of information, there will be a significant up-front cost of developing the technology necessary to provide the necessary records. Whether this requires an investment in the internal development of the technology or the expenditure of licensing a third-party vendor to perform the disclosure, the change will increase the transaction cost or overhead borne by a broker – an effect that could necessitate increased broker margins.  3. The nature of the Rule contradicts Congressional intent.  Finally, the NPRM, in its commentary, says the Agency has revised the requirements of §371.3(c) to make it clear that §371.3(c) is a regulatory requirement –  not a right subject to waiver.  This contradicts the intent of Congress as recognized and correctly stated by the Agency in the NPRM: However, as a general principle, parties are permitted to waive any right unless Congress, by statute, specifically makes a right non-waivable. The Agency has not identified any statutory provision in which Congress expressly barred waivers in this context, and therefore the Agency has not included the requested language in the revised regulation.   NPRM at 91654. The NPRM suggests in one place it is a non-waivable regulatory requirement, but in multiple other places it discusses “if” the proposed rule will change the prevalence of waivers – suggesting that waivers may still be used. The NPRM suggests that a broker who is not willing and able to comply with this proposed rule might be denied the ability to operate in the interstate transportation industry; “it is not a right that can be contracted away, but instead is a regulatory compliance requirement that brokers must meet in order to operate in the interstate transportation industry.”  NPRM at 91660.    Circle Logistics strongly believes waivers should be permitted unless Congress determines otherwise. When 49 CFR §371.3(c) was formalized in the Federal Register decades ago, brokers were paid a percentile commission from the motor carrier to identify freight to be carried. This regulation was put into place so that the motor carrier could verify that the commission paid was correct per its agreement with the broker. Today, brokers are not commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, rather, brokers pay carriers regardless of the rate the shipper pays the broker, so the need to verify commissions no longer exists.   Not only does the need to verify commissions no longer exist, but it contradicts the intended purpose of the broker transaction as described by the ICC as commentary to the Federal Register in May 12, 1980 (emphasis added):  The amount of the broker’s fee is not regulated by the Commission. This means that a broker must engage in a bargaining process with its principals. The amount of commission that a principal agrees to pay will vary according to the benefits it perceives it will gain from the transaction. No party is obligated to deal with a broker or pays its commissions. A party may either choose to do without the broker’s services or to look for another broker who will offer the service at a lower price. In this regard, we note that the property broker industry is a highly competitive one. Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.  Clearly the ICC did not believe in unnecessary regulation, and in fact, vehemently opposed it. The proposed rule is an unnecessary regulation requested by carriers who accept freight for a specific rate and then want to complain about the rate they accepted. Carriers have been arguing broker commissions are too high since at least 1949, when carriers sought a cap on broker commissions to address concerns over alleged excesses.  Ex Parte MC-39, 49 MCC 277 (May 16, 1949), and 14 FR 2833, May 28, 1949 relying on Ex Parte MC-39.  The ICC consistently rejected these challenges and refused to institute a cap on broker commissions.  In 1949, and decades thereafter, shippers typically had transportation departments.  For years, more than two floors in the Sears Tower in Chicago housed Sears’ transportation department.  Now, the days of dedicated transportation departments are long gone, and the broker has effectively become an outsourced transportation department tasked with connecting shipper freight with available motor carriers.  Through deregulation, “Congress had given the ICC the general mandate to open up the bargaining process between shippers and motor carriers, and it sought to remove unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.  The ICC viewed its revisions to the property broker regulations as consistent with those goals.  The rulemaking put into place regulations that are substantially similar to FMCSA’s current property broker regulations.” NPRM at 91651. Now, however, almost 45 years after the Motor Carrier Act of 1980 and the deregulation desired by Congress, the Agency wishes to impose fresh new, useless regulations on brokers.  It is hard to see why this is necessary, or how the Agency believes it has this power to act in contravention of Congressional intent in a post-Chevron era.  Circle Logistics hopes that, after the Agency reviews this comment respectfully submitted by Circle Logistics, the Agency will withdraw the proposed rule, or that it will otherwise limit the same so brokers are only required to disclose information related to a specific dispute on a specific shipment.  Any other action is a significant overreach of Agency authority without justification in fact or law.  4. Impact on illegal brokering.  The Agency asks for comments on eight specific questions in the NPRM. Most are addressed in the body of this response.  However, one deserves specific attention. The Agency suggests that this rule may somehow impact the significant and substantial problem of illegal brokerage in the industry and asks “If this proposal effectively reduced instances of illegal brokering, through carrier policing with transparency information, would the brokers engaged in illegal practices exit the market, resulting in the transfer of illicit profits to legally operating motor carriers and/or brokers.”  The very question shows the Agency has lost touch with what is happening in the industry it regulates.  The most prevalent illegal activity in the transportation industry currently involves imposter brokers or carriers, pretending to be brokers or carriers so they can steal the freight charges on shipments, the shipment itself, or both.  This activity will not be impacted at all by a requirement that certain information be exchanged after the fact.  Likewise, if someone without brokerage authority engages in brokerage in violation of the Moving Ahead for Progress in the 21st Century Act (“MAP-21”), they aren’t going to believe this proposed regulation applies to them – as they would not be a “broker”.  This proposed regulation is incredibly unlikely to impact illegal activity at all, and the suggestion by the Agency that it might help is disheartening.
